coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of porous pinecone-like structure via facile carbon quantum dots modulation: A promising approach for improving the photocatalytic capability of carbon nitride

coredata.dc:description: The modification of photocatalyst morphology and structure have significant influence on their photocatalytic activity. Herein, a novel porous pinecone-like structure carbon quantum dots modified supramolecular self-assembly carbon nitride (N-CQDs/MCNx) with controllable morphology was synthesized by adding carbon quantum dots into Melamine-Cyanuric acid self-assembly system. The characterizations of physicochemical properties demonstrate that the carbon quantum dots affect the hydrogen bond strength between supramolecular self-assembled aggregates by carrying a large number of oxygen-containing groups, resulting in different changes in the final morphology of photocatalysts. In addition, photocatalysts exhibit excellent degradation rate of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) via increasing reaction active sites, enhancing light absorption, improving photogenerated carrier migration rate and inhibiting photogenerated electron-hole recombination. Under the optimum addition of carbon quantum dots, N-CQDs/MCN1 degraded 89% of TC-HCl within 120 min, which was 72% and 49% higher than that of pure carbon nitride (17%) and supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitride (40%), respectively. The trapping experiments further proved that superoxide radicals and holes are the main active species during the photocatalytic degradation of TC-HCl. This research provides a new strategy for the regulation of photocatalyst morphology.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of porous pinecone-like structure via facile carbon quantum dots modulation: A promising approach for improving the photocatalytic capability of carbon nitride

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The modification of photocatalyst morphology and structure have significant influence on their photocatalytic activity. Herein, a novel porous pinecone-like structure carbon quantum dots modified supramolecular self-assembly carbon nitride (N-CQDs/MCNx) with controllable morphology was synthesized by adding carbon quantum dots into Melamine-Cyanuric acid self-assembly system. The characterizations of physicochemical properties demonstrate that the carbon quantum dots affect the hydrogen bond strength between supramolecular self-assembled aggregates by carrying a large number of oxygen-containing groups, resulting in different changes in the final morphology of photocatalysts. In addition, photocatalysts exhibit excellent degradation rate of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) via increasing reaction active sites, enhancing light absorption, improving photogenerated carrier migration rate and inhibiting photogenerated electron-hole recombination. Under the optimum addition of carbon quantum dots, N-CQDs/MCN1 degraded 89% of TC-HCl within 120 min, which was 72% and 49% higher than that of pure carbon nitride (17%) and supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitride (40%), respectively. The trapping experiments further proved that superoxide radicals and holes are the main active species during the photocatalytic degradation of TC-HCl. This research provides a new strategy for the regulation of photocatalyst morphology.

ce:section.ce:para: The effective utilization of solar energy is one of the directions for us to solve the energy problem. To be specific, photocatalysis technology can directly convert solar energy into chemical energy to alleviate the energy crisis, while promoting the light-driven chemical process to degrade pollutants so as to settle the matter of environmental pollution [1]. In a variety of photocatalytic materials, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is widely applied in the research of both pollutant degradation [2,3] and photocatalytic hydrogen production [4], due to its suitable visible light-driven band gap (2.7 eV), excellent chemical stability, low cost and easy synthesis. However, the inherent shortcomings of this material, including the small specific surface area, high recombination rate of photogenerated electrons-holes, low charge mobility and poor visible light absorption capacity [5,6], limit its application. So far, in many studies, attempts have been made to optimize the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 by constructing heterojunctions [7–9], adjusting morphology [10], doping with single-atom precious metal or non-metal elements [11], as well as increasing defects [12].

The adjustment of the surface morphology with the metal-free photocatalyst g-C3N4 is an important method to improve its photocatalytic performance. Generally, the morphology of g-C3N4 can be adjusted by template method [13] and supramolecular self-assembly [14]. The latter method has attracted the attentions of the researchers in the field of photocatalysis due to its advantages, such as easy operation, mild reaction conditions and orderly structure [15]. Supramolecular self-assembled g-C3N4 means that different precursors, such as melamine, transform small molecules into highly ordered supramolecular aggregates via specific interactions. Supramolecular aggregates form g-C3N4 with different morphologies such as tubular, porous nanosheet and petal ball, by means of through thermal polymerization, which is achieved by changing precursors, using solvents and heating conditions [16–18]. However, in many studies [19,20], there are still some urgent problems that need to be solved in the preparation of g-C3N4 by supramolecular self-assembly. For example, supramolecular aggregates with special morphologies will be destroyed after calcination. Thus, how to maintain the special morphologies of the supramolecular aggregates has become the focus of the research. And enhancing the hydrogen bond interaction between supramolecular aggregates is considered to be an effective method. Chen et al. [21] found that bridging the benzene ring with a hydroxyl group in the supramolecular carbon nitride can strengthen the hydrogen bonding between each other, so that the supramolecular aggregates retain the original morphology. According to Huang et al. [22], the addition of cyclodextrin molecules containing polyhydroxy groups to the precursors can effectively change the number and the strength of the hydrogen bonds between the supramolecular self-assembled aggregates. Therefore, the introduction of polyhydroxy groups into the precursors of supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitride is expected to control the formation of morphology by regulating hydrogen bonds.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are a kind of new carbon-based nanomaterials with the size of less than 10 nm, possesses many oxygen-containing groups are on the surface and show unique properties [23,24]. In many works, CQDs have been successfully deposited on the surface of g-C3N4 or combined with g-C3N4 through covalent bonds. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic activity of pure g-C3N4 has been effectively improved by broadening the absorption range of the visible light, enhancing the absorption of the visible light, and improving the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers [25–36]. Moreover, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) can further improve electron transfer and expand the absorption region of the visible light by doping with N atoms [37]. In the report on CQDs modified g-C3N4, the function of CQDs has not been clarified clearly. In addition to the photoelectric studies, the effect of CQDs modulation on the material morphology has not been researched systematically. In recent years, antibiotics have caught the widespread attention due to their potential hazards to both aquatic ecosystem and human health [38]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop efficient and environmentally compatible photocatalysts so as to degrade antibiotics.

In this work, N-CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method were introduced into the melamine-cyanuric acid supramolecular aggregate (MCA). Through further calcination, the N-CQDs were successfully bridged to supramolecular self-assembly g-C3N4 (MCN) via covalent bond, and the porous pinecone-like structure N-CQDs/MCNx was prepared with success. Detailed characterization showed that the obtained photocatalyst N-CQDs/MCNx maintained the pinecone-like morphology of MCA after the incorporation of N-CQDs, and the change of morphology was related to the contents of N-CQDs. The special morphology increased the specific surface area of the photocatalyst, providing more active sites. Meanwhile, the optical and electrochemical properties were improved through the characteristics of N-CQDs, which effectively enhanced the photocatalytic performance for tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) under the visible light. Furthermore, the incorporation of N-CQDs did not introduce additional elements, which made the catalyst retain the original metal-free and pollution-free characteristics. Thus, this work offers inspiration for the development of other materials with excellent performance and special morphology.

All materials were analytical grade and used as received without further treatment. Melamine and cyanuric acid purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), ethanol absolute, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Citric acid and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) were supplied by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Urea and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corp, P. R. China.

The N-CQDs were synthesized via a modified hydrothermal method [39]. Typically, 6.0 g of citric acid and 2.0 g of urea were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water. After ultrasonic for 30 min, the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained brown solution was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 30 min, and then the supernatant was placed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The resulting solution was stored in the dark at 4 ℃ for subsequent use. Common carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by the same steps without adding urea.

Bulk g-C3N4 (BCN) was synthesized by thermal polymerization. 3.0 g melamine was put into a covered alumina crucible, and then calcined in a muffle furnace at 550 ℃ for 3 h (heating rate of 5 ℃/min). After cooling to room temperature, the product was ground, and the obtained yellow powder was denoted as BCN.

Briefly, 1 g melamine was dissolved in 40 mL DMSO, denoted as solution A. 1.02 g cyanic acid and appropriate amount of N-CQDs solution were dissolved in 40 mL DMSO, denoted as solution B. Solution A and B were stirred for 1 h, then ultrasonic treatment for 30 min to mix uniformly. The white suspension was obtained after solution A and B were mixed, and the suspension stood for 2 h. The white suspended matter was washed several times with ethanol and deionized water, then dried to powder at 60 ℃. Transfer the white powder to an alumina crucible with a cover, and continue heating at 550 ℃ for 3 h (heating rate is 5 ℃/min). The light yellow powder collected after cooling to room temperature is the composite photocatalyst N-CQDs/MCNx (x = 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3 mL of N-CQDs solution). Use the same steps to add CQDs solution to obtain CQDs/MCNx for comparison. Supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitride (MCN) is prepared in the same way as above method without adding N-CQDs or CQDs solution.

The morphologies were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-3400 N) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2800). The crystalline phases of the samples were characterized on an X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, D/MAX 2500 V, Japan) with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å) irradiation at 40 kV and 100 mA. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with IRTracer-100 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted by a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha Nexsa equipped with standard monochromatic Al Kα excitation source. The optical properties of samples were investigated through a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis DRS, UV-3600Plus) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (PL, FL3C-111). Electron paramagnanetic resonance (EPR) signals were obtained with a Bruker EMXplus.

In order to evaluated the photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples, a 300 W xenon lamp (cut-off filter < 420 nm) was used as the visible light source,and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) was selected as the target pollutant. Specifically, 25 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL (10 mg/L) TC-HCl solution and stirred in the dark for 30 min before illumination to achieved adsorption-desorption equilibrium. When the light turned on, extracted about 4 mL of reaction suspension at a fixed interval of 30 min, filtered it through a 0.22 µm membrane filter, and measured the concentration by an UV–vis spectrophotometer at 357 nm.

The quenching experiments were employed to ascertain the role of various kinds of reactive species in the TC-HCl photodegradaton process. BQ, IPA and EDTA-2Na were added to the pollutant solution as scavengers for superoxide radical (·O2 -), hydroxyl radical (·OH) and hole (h+), respectively [40,41].

The photoelectrochemical properties of the samples were measured on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) with Ag/AgCl were used as the reference electrode [31]. The working electrode was obtained by dip-coating method. A certain amount of catalyst powder was dispersed in 2 mL of anhydrous ethanol to prepare suspension, which was then coated onto a FTO glass electrode. Furthermore, the electrolyte used in this experiment was 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution.

The synthesis process of porous pinecone-like structure N-CQDs/MCNx is shown in Scheme 1. After cyanuric acid and melamine were mixed, the hexagonal aggregate MCA was formed through supramolecular self-assembly. In addition, under the action of DMSO, the hexagonal aggregates were continuously stacked into a pinecone shape. At the same time, N-CQDs were loaded on MCA. During the calcination process, the bridge between N-CQDs and MCA was constructed by forming C－O－C covalent bonds. More importantly, due to the various oxygen-containing groups on the surface of N-CQDs, the strength, direction, and number of hydrogen bonds between melamine and cyanuric acid were changed, which affected the morphology of MCN after thermal polymerization.

The crystal structure of the photocatalyst was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1a, all samples contain two characteristic diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 (JCPDS No. 87–1526), indicating that all samples present the crystal framework of carbon nitride. The two peaks at 12.8° and 27.3° correspond to the (100) and (002) crystal plane, which are assigned to in-plane repeated tri-s-triazine units and interlayer stacking structure conjugated aromatic system, respectively [42,43]. The BCN shows two diffraction peaks with high intensity, while the diffraction peaks intensity of MCN and N-CQDs/MCNx decrease significantly, indicating that the inherent ordered structure of carbon nitride was damaged and the crystallinity decreased. It can be observed that compared with MCN, the (002) diffraction peak of N-CQDs/MCNx gradually moves to the right with the increase of N-CQDs content. It is suggested that the addition of N-CQDs enhances the interlayer force of MCN, so the interlayer spacing gradually decreases [44]. Fig. 1b shows the FT-IR spectra of composite photocatalysts with different N-CQDs content, and the signal belongs to s-triazine unit at 810 cm−1 [45,46]. Several peaks observed in the range of 1200 − 1700 cm−1 are typical tensile vibrations of C－N heterocycles, and the broad peak at 3000 − 3600 cm−1 is attributed to N－H and O－H [47]. The above results show that both the MCN synthesized by supramolecular self-assembly and the MCN modified with N-CQDs (or CQDs) maintain the basic chemical structure of BCN. Moreover, due to the relatively low content of N-CQDs or CQDs, no characteristic peaks related to quantum dots are observed in XRD and FT-IR.

In order to study the effect of carbon quantum dots on MCN, the morphology of photocatalyst is observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM). The precursor MCA self-assembled by cyanuric acid and melamine is shown in Fig. 2a. The MCA is a uniform pinecone-like structure with an average size of 4–6 µm and is composed of a stack of hexagonal nanosheets. The BCN formed by thermal polymerization of melamine is a block structure as shown in Fig. 2b. MCN (Fig. 2c) was synthesized by calcining the precursor MCA. It is observed that the inherent pinecone-like morphology of precursor MCA is destroyed and folded nanosheets are formed. There are many pore structures in the nanosheets, which could be assigned to the generation of ammonia gas during the thermal polymerization process [20]. After the addition of N-CQDs, N-CQDs/MCNx (Fig. 2d-f) maintained the pinecone-like structure of MCA, and the morphology remained more complete with the increase of N-CQDs content. The morphology of CQDs/MCN1 added with CQDs is shown in Fig. S1. According to the SEM results, adding an appropriate amount of carbon quantum dots can maintain the original morphology structure of supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitride. It is speculated that the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of carbon quantum dots affect the strength of hydrogen bonds between supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitrides, strengthen the hydrogen bonds between layers, and support the structure to maintain the original morphology and avoid structural damage [21].

To further explore the morphology of the samples, TEM was also performed. The TEM image of N-CQDs shows in Fig. 3a, the average size of quantum dots is about 2 nm and relatively stable, which proves that carbon quantum dots with uniform particle size and small size have been successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method. The TEM image of MCA is shown in Fig. 3b, MCA presents a regular spherical structure. MCN formed by direct calcination of MCA is a disorderly crimped stack of ultrathin nanosheets (Fig. S2). Fig. 3c is N-CQDs/MCN1, which shows the pinecone-like sphere composed of porous flakes, and NCQDs are uniformly modified on the flakes (Fig. 3d). HRTEM image shows the N-CQDs modified on the MCN, marked by red circles, and the spacing of 0.23 nm which corresponds to the graphite phase (100) crystal plane of the N-CQDs could be observed [48]. The N-CQDs/MCN1 energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) element map is used to analyze the distribution of component elements. As shown in Fig. S3, C, N and O elements are evenly distributed throughout the sample. In addition to the water molecules adsorbed by the sample, the O element comes from the oxygen-containing groups of N-CQDs. The above results prove that N-CQDs are successfully modified on the MCN layer.

The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the as-prepared samples are investigated by N2 adsorption desorption isotherm. In Fig. S4a-b, all samples show type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, indicating the presence of mesopores [49]. As shown in Table 1, compared to BCN (5.5 m2g−1), the specific surface area of MCN and N-CQDs/MCNx are greatly improved. The increased specific surface area is due to the transformation from the block structure to the spherical structure of nanosheet stacking. After adding 0.5 mL, 1 mL and 3 mL of N-CQDs, the BET surface areas of the N-CQDs/MCNx samples increase first and then decrease, reaching 90.82, 108.57 and 103.73 m2g−1 respectively, which are higher than MCN (79.62 m2g−1), about 16 times, 20 times and 18 times higher than that of BCN. In addition, Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) analysis shows that the pore size of N-CQDs/MCNx is mainly distributed in the range of 2–60 nm, while BCN has almost no porous structure. The porous structure created with ammonia released by during carbon nitride polymerization and CO2 released by decomposition of N-CQDs. With the increase of N-CQDs, the number of mesopores also increase first and then decrease, which corresponds to the change of morphology from loose to tight in SEM (Fig. 2a-f). Thus, all the above analyses indicated that N-CQDs introduction along with its addition amount played vital roles in controlling the microstructures of the samples. Adding too many or too little N-CQDs could result in faiure to form a loose porous structure. Too little N-CQDs content makes the spherical structure of the sample incomplete and the number of mesopores is small, while too much N-CQDs content makes the sample structure compact and the specific surface area becomes smaller. Generally, the loose porous structure would give the sample with large specific surface area [50], so N-CQDs/MCN1 possessed the largest specific surface area among all the samples. The increase of specific surface area means more reactive sites, and the porous structure can enhance the light absorption.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the surface chemical composition and elemental state of photocatalyst. The XPS spectra in Fig. 4a shows that MCN and N-CQDs/MCN1 are mainly composed of C, N and O, and no impurity elements are detected. According to Table S1, after adding N-CQDs, the atomic ratio of C and N (C/N) increase from 0.83 to 0.85, which is consistent with the C/N results measured by the element analyzer (Table S2). In order to prove the successful doping of N-CQDs, the C1s spectra of the photocatalysts were further analyzed (Fig. 4b). It can be observed that the photocatalyst has two main characteristic peaks of 284.8 eV and 288.2 eV, corresponding to C－C and sp2 carbon bonded to a N atom inside the triazine ring (N－C＝N) respectively [47], indicating that the incorporation of N-CQDs has no effect on the basic structure of carbon nitride. More importantly, after the introduction of N-CQDs, a new peak at 287.1 eV could be observed in the C 1s spectra of N-CQDs/MCN1, which corresponds to the C－OH bond on the N-CQDs group [51,52]. As shown in N 1s (Fig. 4c), the three characteristic peaks at 398.7 eV, 399.8 eV, and 404.9 eV are fitted to C－N＝C, tertiary nitrogen (N－(C)3) and π-π * excited charge effect, respectively [50]. At the same time, thus to the effects of N-CQDs on the chemical environment and electronic structure of the MCN surface, the binding energy has a slight shift of about 0.1–0.2 eV [53]. As for the O 1s spectra (Fig. 4d), the characteristic peak at 532.4 eV is attributable to the adsorbed water or oxygen on the MCN [31]. In addition, a new peak at 531.1 eV appeared in the spectra of N-CQDs/MCN1, which corresponds to the O element of C－O bond, and further proves the successful doping of N-CQDs [54]. The results of XPS indicate that the N-CQDs are successfully connected to the surface of MCN through C－O－C bonds [52,53]. It is speculated that electrons in MCN are transferred to N-CQDs through covalent bonds C－O－C [25].

The optical properties of MCN, CQDs/MCN1 and N-CQDs/MCNx were researched via UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis DRS). As shown in Fig. 5a, the absorption spectrum of BCN shows a typical absorption band, the absorption edge is about 456 nm, and the calculated band gap (Eg) is about 2.65 eV, which is consistent with the reported work [55]. Compared with BCN, the absorption edge of MCN has a slight red shift, and the light absorption intensity in the visible light region is greatly improved, which could be ascribed to change of carbon nitride from a bulk structure to a porous nanosheet structure [18,56]. It is worth noting that N-CQDs modification has a smaller effect on the absorption edge while compared with MCN. But as the content of N-CQDs increases, the light absorption intensity in the visible light region shows a trend of first increasing and then decreasing, and the light absorption in the long wavelength region is slightly enhanced, which is caused by the unique morphology structure of N-CQDs/MCNx and the up-conversion characteristics of N-CQDs [29,39]. Compared with CQDs/MCN1, the light absorption of N-CQDs/MCN1 has a certain enhancement, indicating that the fluorescence produced by N-CQDs is brighter than CQDs, which is consistent with Fig. S5. From the electronic Eg derived from the Tauc plots, the corresponding Eg energies of BCN, MCN, N-CQDs/MCN1 are 2.65, 2.61 and 2.53 eV, respectively (Fig. 5a illustration). UV–vis DRS results prove that the enhanced light absorption attributed to the unique porous pinecone-like morphology of N-CQDs/MCNx, which enables incident light to be reflected multiple times in the material, and the up-conversion characteristics of N-CQDs can convert incident light with longer wavelengths into the carbon nitride matrix can absorb the shorter wavelength light, which broadens the light absorption range. However, massive N-CQDs will cause a shading effect and hinder the light absorption of MCN.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the photocatalysts can be used to measure the electron-hole recombination rate. The lower the electron-hole recombination rate is beneficial to the photocatalytic activity [57,58]. As shown in Fig. 5b, the PL spectrum of BCN was excited by a laser source with a wavelength of 365 nm, showing a strong emission peak at about 450 nm, indicating a high electron-hole recombination rate. Compared with BCN, the intensity of emission peaks of MCN, CQDs/MCN1 and N-CQDs/MCNx are significantly reduced, indicating that the special morphology different from the bulk can inhibit electron-hole recombination [39,59]. In other words, electrons can transfer through the covalent bond C－O－C between N-CQDs and MCN in N-CQDs/MCNx samples, which will enhance the separation and the transfer of photo-induced charge carries. The emission peak intensity of the CQDs/MCN1 sample is slightly higher than that of the N-CQDs/MCN1, implying that the N-CQDs has a stronger ability to capture electrons than the CQDs. The lower fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs/MCN1 is due to the combined effect of special morphology, increased specific surface area and quantum dot doping.

The charge carrier migration behavior of the photocatalyst through photoelectrochemical research [60]. As shown in Fig. 6a, N-CQDs/MCN1 shows the largest photocurrent response under visible light irradiation, which is significantly higher than that of BCN. In addition, after five on-off lighting cycles, the transient photocurrents of all photocatalysts are reversible and reproducible, indicating that the samples have good photoelectrochemical stability. Simultaneously, the arc radius of N-CQDs/MCN1 in electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is also smaller than that of BCN and MCN (Fig. 6b). The smaller the arc radius, the smaller the photogenerated carrier transfer barrier and the higher the conductivity [61]. This results further reveal the separation and transfer efficiency of the electron-hole pairs of the sample is N-CQDs/MCN1 > MCN > BCN, which is consistent with the previous characterization. To investigate the electronic structure of N-CQDs/MCN1, Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots was performed to the photocatalysts. In Fig. 6c, the positive slope of M-S curve confirms that the samples are n-type semiconductors [7]. The flat potentials of BCN，MCN and N-CQDs/MCN1 are determined to be − 0.77, − 0.85 and − 1.02 V (νs. Ag/AgCl), respectively, which corresponded to − 0.55, − 0.63 and − 0.80 V (νs. NHE). For n-type semiconductors, it is generally considered that the flat-band potential (EFB) is close to its conduction band (CB) potential [31]. Therefore, the valence band (VB) positions of the BCN, MCN and N-CQDs/MCN1 are calculated to be 2.1, 1.98 and 1.73 eV, based on their Eg and CB (EVB = Eg + ECB). Fig. 6d conveys the energy band structures of photocatalysts BCN, MCN and N-CQDs/MCN1. The more negative CB indicates the stronger the reduction of excited e-, and the narrower the band gap indicates the stronger the light absorption capacity of photocatalysis [43,62,63]. Thus, the better photocatalytic activity of photocatalyst N-CQDs/MCN1 can be predicted, which is realized by its special morphology and N-CQDs modification.

Under the condition of pH = 7 of aqueous solution, the photocatalytic activity of photocatalyst N-CQDs/MCNx was evaluated by degrading tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) with visible light. The dark reaction experiment was carried out by stirring in the dark for 2 h (Fig. S6a), which proved that the samples reached the adsorption desorption equilibrium in 1 h. In Fig. 7a, the pollutants cannot be degraded without the addition of a photocatalyst. After all samples are stirred in the dark for 60 min, the adsorption of pollutants by MCN and N-CQDs/MCNx are enhanced compared with BCN, because MCN and N-CQDs/MCNx prepared by supramolecular self-assembly method had large specific surface area. After turning on the xenon lamp, N-CQDs/MCN1 degrades 89% of TC-HCl within 120 min, which is 72% and 49% higher than that of BCN (17%) and MCN (40%), respectively. As the amount of N-CQDs added increases from 0.5 to 3 mL, the degradation rate of the N-CQDs/MCNx shows a trend of first increasing and then decreasing, and the N-CQDs/MCN1 sample achieves the best degradation efficiency. The reason is that the excess N-CQDs will cover the surface of the MCN, play a role in shading, and compete with MCN to absorb photons, resulting in hindered light absorption. In addition, MCA has almost no adsorption effect and photocatalytic activity for pollutants. As shown in Fig. S6b, the pollutant TC-HCl always degrades stably with the extension of illumination time. In addition, according to the experimental results of photocatalytic degradation of TC-HCl at different pH (Fig. S6c), we found that when pH = 7, the photocatalytic effect is the best, which is more conducive to the application of photocatalysts in the actual water environment. Similarly, the degradation rate of photocatalyst CQDs/MCNx also has the same trend (Fig. S7a). Photocatalytic experiments show that MCN has better photocatalytic performance than BCN, because the morphology of the photocatalyst is stripped from blocks into folded nanosheets, and the increased specific surface area can expose more active sites. Moreover, after the modification of N-CQDs, N-CQDs/MCNx (CQDs/MCNx) further improves the photocatalytic performance of MCN because of the synergistic effects of regulating morphology, enhancing light absorption and improving the migration rate of photogenerated carriers. Significantly, the addition of an appropriate amount of carbon quantum dots can not only regulate the morphology and structure of the photocatalyst, but also have a crucial impact on the photocatalytic performance, which corresponds to the previous characterization results. As shown in Fig. 7b, the kinetic rate constant of degradation of TC-HCl by N-CQDs/MCNx were further explored. Compared with BCN, the rate constants of MCN and N-CQDs/MCNx increase significantly. With the increase of the amount of N-CQDs, the k value first increase and then decrease. Optimal activity is obtained with N-CQDs/MCN1 (0.0165 min−1), which is represented 12.7 times higher than that of BCN (0.00129 min−1). As shown in Table S3, compared with other reported g-C3N4-based photocatalysts, N-CQDs/MCN1 not only have excellent photodegradation activity, but also can control the morphology.

Total organic carbon (TOC) was used to measure the extent of mineralization during photocatalytic degradation of TC-HCl ( Fig. 8a). In 120 min, N-CQDs/MCN1 could degrad 87% of TC-HCl, while only 52% of TOC was removed. This result indicates that in the degradation process of TC-HCl, other intermediate products are generated first, and then decomposed into CO2 and H2O [31]. As shown in Fig. 8b, after four cycles of cyclic photocatalytic reaction, the degradation rate of TC-HCl decreased slightly, from 88% to 82%. No distinct change of XRD patterns and FT-IR patterns is found for the recycled samples (Fig. S8), indicating the excellent stability of the N-CQDs/MCN1, which is a key factor whether the photocatalyst can be applied to practice.

In order to investigate the main reactive species in the photocatalytic process, BQ, IPA and EDTA-2Na were selected as quenchers of ·O2 -, ·OH and h+, respectively. In Fig. 9a, great inhibitions occurred when BQ and EDTA-2Na were added, which implies that the ·O2 - and h+ played dominant roles in TC-HCl degradation. Nevertheless, the TC-HCl degradation rate decrease slightly with the addition of IPA, implying that ·OH is a minor contributor for the degradation of TC-HCl. The electron paramagnanetic resonance (EPR) was used to identify the formation of radical, presented in Fig. 9b and c. In the N-CQDs/MCN1 photocatalytic system, no relevant signal was detected under dark conditions, proving that ·O2 - and ·OH did not exist before the light was turned on. As expected, DMPO molecules quickly react with ·O2 - and form DMPO-·O2 -, a stable EPR active product, when exposed to constant visible light [31,64]. In addition, no signal related to DMPO-·OH was detected in visible light, which proves that no ·OH was produced. The above results indicate that ·O2 - is the main reactive species in N-CQDs/MCN1 photocatalytic system, and there is no ·OH formation in the reaction system, which depended on the energy band structure of the catalyst, it will be summarized in the later part.

Based on the above discussion, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of N-CQDs/MCN1 on TC-HCL is proposed ( Fig. 10). When the photocatalyst was exposed to visible light irradiaton, the incident light is reflected multiple times in the spherical porous structure of N-CQDs/MCN1, and the electrons on valence band (VB) are excited to conduction band (CB) under the excitation of light greater than the band gap energy. Subsequently, the e- on CB are transferred to N-CQDs through C－O－C covalent bond, allowing effective separation of electron-holes pairs. Then, due to the CB of N-CQDs/MCN1 is − 0.80 eV (νs. NHE), which is more negative than the redox potential of O2/·O2 - (−0.33 eV νs. NHE), the electrons on N-CQDs react with oxygen on the catalyst surface to form ·O2 - [33,41]. Meanwhile, as the VB position of N-CQDs/MCN1 (1.73 eV) is more negative than the standard redox potential of·OH/OH- (1.99 eV νs. NHE), the h+ from N-CQDs/MCN1 were opposed to oxidizing OH- into ·OH. Therefore, corresponding to the quenching experiment of active species and the results of the EPR, ·OH has almost no effect in this photocatalytic reaction. Finally, the TC-HCl would be more easily absorbed to the surface of the N-CQDs/MCN1, and the photocatalytic efficiency is enhanced due to the large specific surface area provides abundant active sites. Then, the TC-HCl were attacked by the ·O2 - and h+, leading to the pollutants decomposed and until complete mineralized.

In summary, in this paper, a novel N-CQDs/MCNx photocatalyst with controllable morphology was synthesized by utilizing supramolecular self-assembly method. The photocatalyst shows a uniform porous pinecone-like structure. At the same time, by adjusting the contents of N-CQDs, the hydrogen bond strength between the materials can be effectively controlled, so as to control the final morphology of the samples. Among them, the N-CQDs/MCN1 sample possesses the most excellent photocatalytic activity, and the rate constant exhibited is 12.7 times and 4 times larger than that of BCN and MCN, respectively. The enhanced photocatalytic activity benefits from the synergy of its porous pinecone-like structure and N-CQDs modification. Specifically, the former enhances the reflection of incident light and expands the specific surface area so as to increase the number of reactive sites. While, the latter effectively transfers electrons, enhances the migration rate of photogenerated carriers, and inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. In short, this paper may provide some research directions for the future researchers. Because the potential of carbon quantum dots not only involves the photoelectric properties, but also the exploration on the modulation capacity of their morphology and microstructure, which can provide a new idea for the synthesis of photocatalysts with special morphology.
